summary: The user, a public works engineer, investigated the root causes of poor contractor performance, concluding that systemic issues like an aging tipper fleet (8.1-year average) and a significant INR 9.80 crore fleet procurement budget under-spend are the primary drivers. The user connected the catastrophic failure of contractor GreenHaul—evidenced by a 34% manpower shortfall and low segregation rates (19-27%)—directly to the city's highest citizen complaint rate (28.4/1000 households in Zone 11) and the impending landfill crisis. While validating the technical choice for the proposed Waste-to-Energy (WtE) plant, the user identified a critical new risk: its 2,000 TPD capacity relies on 75% source segregation, as its on-site pre-processing hall is only 500 TPD. This finding solidified the user's position that the WtE project is not plausible without first fixing fundamental services. The user's stated strategy remains to terminate GreenHaul, resolve procurement delays to upgrade the fleet, and stabilize collection to improve the city-wide 41% segregation rate as a prerequisite for any long-term solution.
key_facts: The city's tipper truck fleet has an average age of 8.1 years.
The Fleet & Equipment budget was under-spent by INR 9.80 crore due to procurement delays.
Contractor GreenHaul has a 34% manpower shortfall and has been penalized INR 1.03 crore.
The contract termination process requires a 90-day cure notice under Clause 22.4.
The city is spending INR 2.40 crore from contingency funds to cover GreenHaul's service shortfalls.
Zone 11, serviced by GreenHaul, has the city's highest complaint rate at 28.4 per 1,000 households.
The Pallavaram landfill has an estimated 8 months of remaining lifespan.
The proposed WtE plant has a 2,000 TPD capacity, but its pre-processing hall can only handle 500 TPD.
The city's current overall waste segregation rate is 41%.
The WtE plant's moving grate technology is suitable for the city's segregated waste calorific value (1,280 kcal/kg).
positions_taken: Contractor performance gaps are a symptom of the city's own procurement failures and aging equipment.
The public health crisis is a direct result of root-cause engineering and operational failures in the waste collection system.
The WtE plant's success is entirely dependent on fixing the collection and segregation system first, a risk magnified by its limited on-site processing capacity.
Consistent, reliable collection service is the key to improving public participation in waste segregation.
The user's strategic priority is to terminate GreenHaul, fix fleet procurement, and stabilize basic services before the WtE project can be considered plausible.
